coredata.dc:title: Lithium-ion battery model parametrisation: BatPar an all-in-one toolkit for equivalent circuit models
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Tao
Tomlin, Ruben
Garcia, Carlos
Rawat, Sunil
Holland, Thomas
Offer, Gregory
Marinescu, Monica
coredata.dc:description: Accurate models of batteries are crucial for understanding their performance, safety, and longevity in applications ranging from mobile devices to electric vehicles. Equivalent Circuit Models (ECMs) are indispensable in the design and optimisation of battery systems, as they provide a balance between computational speed and the ability to represent the dynamic response. The model accuracy in predicting real behaviour is critically limited by the parametrisation process. While many articles describe the experiments required for parametrisation, fewer describe the process of parameter estimation from the data. This work introduces BatPar - a comprehensive open-source MATLAB-based toolkit designed to enhance the efficiency and reliability of parametrisation for equivalent circuit models for battery cells. BatPar offers unparalleled adaptability to a wide range of experimental protocols and is compatible with both full-cell and half-cell models. BatPar stands out for its advanced capability to handle parameter dependencies on temperature and current, its user-friendly graphical interface, and its advanced features, such as batch processing, quality diagnosis of parameters, and advanced post-processing. With its extensive user and developer manuals, BatPar aims to streamline the process of battery parametrisation, contributing to advancements in energy storage system management and optimisation strategies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery
Parametrisation
Equivalent circuit model
Graphical user Interface
Half-cell model

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid fundingbodyid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder sponsoredaccesstype srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the Innovate UK (Power-up project, grant number 10007479 ; WIZer, grant number 104427 ; GENESIS, grant number 10007488 ), UK Engineering and Physical Science Research Council (Multi-Scale Modelling, grant number EP/S003053/1 ; EPSRC CASE, grant number EP/W524323/1 ), and the Faraday Institution (Multi-Scale Modelling project, grant number FIRG003 , FIRG025 , and FIRG059 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A second-order RC equivalent circuit model.
Flowchart of BatPar and interactions between scripts and functions.
A first-order half-cell equivalent circuit model.
The graphical user interface of BatPar.
Batch processing input table for configuring parametrisation requirements for each dataset.
Example real-time progress of parametrisation displayed in the MATLAB command window.

BatPar's presentation of parametrisation results: (a) Collection of the log file, post-processed table and plots. (b) Tabular presentation of parametrisation results for all parameters. (c) Graphical presentation of parametrisation results for OCV. (d) Comparison between the experimentally measured and the ECM-predicted cell voltage.

BatPar's post-processed plots facilitate users in diagnosing the quality of parameters: (a) The trend of R0 with respect to SOC should approximately exhibit an L-shape. (b) R1 and R2 should not have too many abrupt changes in the mid-range SOC area.

Examples of BatPar extracting parameters from an arbitrary (drive cycle) test: (a) Comparison between the experimentally measured and the ECM-predicted cell voltage. Comparison between parameters extracted from the standard test and those extracted from the arbitrary test - (b) OCV. (c) R0. (d) R1. (e) R2.

Examples of BatPar extracting parameters under complex temperature and/or current experimental conditions: (a) C1 obtained under complex temperature conditions. (b) C2 obtained under complex current conditions. (c) R0 obtained under two different temperatures and four different current conditions.

Examples of BatPar voltage predictions of half-cell ECMs parametrised from PyBAMM half-cell cathode and anode for the LG M50 cell: (a) Comparison between the observed and the simulated voltage for the cathode half-cell. (b) Comparison between the observed and the simulated voltage for the anode half-cell.
Voltage boundary conditions for full-cell/cathode/anode, discharge/charge parametrisation, configured in BatPar.

row.entry: Vmax > Voc > V > Vmin Voc > Vi > 0

head.ce:title: Lithium-ion battery model parametrisation: BatPar an all-in-one toolkit for equivalent circuit models

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Imperial College London, London SW7 2AZ, United Kingdom
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Imperial College London, London SW7 2AZ, United Kingdom

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Warwick Manufacturing Group, University of Warwick, Warwick CV4 7AL, United Kingdom
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Warwick Manufacturing Group, University of Warwick, Warwick, CV4 7AL, United Kingdom

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Faraday Institution, Harwell Science and Innovation Campus, Didcot OX11 0RA, United Kingdom
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Faraday Institution, Harwell Science and Innovation Campus, Didcot OX11 0RA, United Kingdom

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Imperial College London, London SW7 2AZ, United Kingdom.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate models of batteries are crucial for understanding their performance, safety, and longevity in applications ranging from mobile devices to electric vehicles. Equivalent Circuit Models (ECMs) are indispensable in the design and optimisation of battery systems, as they provide a balance between computational speed and the ability to represent the dynamic response. The model accuracy in predicting real behaviour is critically limited by the parametrisation process. While many articles describe the experiments required for parametrisation, fewer describe the process of parameter estimation from the data. This work introduces BatPar - a comprehensive open-source MATLAB-based toolkit designed to enhance the efficiency and reliability of parametrisation for equivalent circuit models for battery cells. BatPar offers unparalleled adaptability to a wide range of experimental protocols and is compatible with both full-cell and half-cell models. BatPar stands out for its advanced capability to handle parameter dependencies on temperature and current, its user-friendly graphical interface, and its advanced features, such as batch processing, quality diagnosis of parameters, and advanced post-processing. With its extensive user and developer manuals, BatPar aims to streamline the process of battery parametrisation, contributing to advancements in energy storage system management and optimisation strategies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I've attached in the manuscript a GitHub link to the source codes and user/developer manuals

ce:section.ce:para: Battery technologies have become an integral part of our daily lives, powering everything from mobile devices to electric vehicles. Batteries undergo complex, temperature-dependent electrochemical processes during operation. Current step has been used as excitation, allowing the battery voltage response to be divided into instantaneous, short and long term response. This allows the response to be fit into an electric circuit comprised of standard electrical components, mainly capacitors and resistors [1]. To understand and improve upon these complex behaviours, one of the cornerstone methodologies is the Equivalent Circuit Model/Modelling (ECM). This approach simplifies the intricate chemical and electrical processes in the battery by representing it as an equivalent circuit, which comprises elements such as voltage sources, resistors, and capacitors [2]. Furthermore, the ECM is designed to streamline the analysis and prediction of batteries' behaviour under various conditions, rendering it particularly well-suited for state estimation in real-time applications in battery management systems [3]. Recent advancements have witnessed various applications of ECMs to batteries, including state estimation [4], thermal management [5], safety monitoring [5], fast charging design [6], ageing and degradation analysis [7], real-time control [8], cost-efficiency analysis [9], environmental impact study [10], etc. As with any model, parametrisation plays a vital role. If successful, the ECM can become, as much as possible, an accurate reflection of the battery's real-world behaviour, ideally emulating both its steady-state and transient characteristics [11]. Due to the intense and diverse use of ECMs for batteries, accurate parameter values are pivotal for estimating key performance metrics such as State-of-Charge (SOC) and State-of-Health (SOH), for ensuring battery safety, for fault diagnosis, and for control strategies, including energy management and charging [12].

The process of parametrisation typically consists of two steps: experimentation and parameter extraction. In the experimentation phase, the battery voltage is measured in response to current pulses, usually used in discharge [13]. Standard experimental protocols like GITT (Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration Technique) [14] and HPPC (Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization) [15] are commonly utilised, both consisting of a series of constant-amplitude current pulses alternating with relaxation (zero-current) periods. In addition to standard protocols, there are variant protocols such as AMPP (Accelerated Model Parametrisation Procedure) [16] and Multi-sine [17]. These protocols utilise variable-amplitude current pulses and either shorten or eliminate relaxation periods, thus reducing the overall experimentation time at the cost of generating a complex voltage response.

Following the experimentation stage, the extraction stage leverages the relationship between the input current and the observed voltage to extract the parameters of ECM elements across different SOCs [18]. Parameter extraction methodologies for batteries can be broadly classified into two categories: analytical methods and numerical methods. Analytical methods primarily rely on solving closed-form equations derived from electrical physics rules on the ECM. These methods typically require the experimentation to follow specific protocols, resulting in data with fixed formats. This reliance on fixed formats means that any deviation in experimental data can lead to complexities in analysis. Consequently, flexibility in data handling becomes limited, making the interpretation of non-standard data formats a significant challenge. Analytical methods offer computational speed advantages, especially for low-order ECMs or high-order models simplified linearly. However, such simplifications may compromise model accuracy [19]. In contrast, numerical methods employ optimisation algorithms to iteratively refine the ECM parameters as non-linear least-squares problems, thereby minimising the discrepancies between observed and simulated battery behaviours [20]. These methods have less stringent requirements for experimental protocols, allowing flexibility in data format. Numerical methods are capable of handling highly non-linear models where closed-form solutions are not possible, providing a more accurate representation of the battery behaviour. However, due to their iterative nature, numerical methods often require substantial computational power [21]. In practice, it is rare for parameter extraction methods to exclusively rely on either analytical or numerical approaches. Most research combines analytical and numerical techniques to varying degrees, aiming to strike a balance between accuracy and speed. Recent advances in parameter extraction methods are reviewed below.

The two most typical parameter extraction methods are likely Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and layered methods. Vyroubal et al. [22] propose a method employing the EIS, which provides insights into the electrochemical and physical processes within batteries. The study emphasises the effectiveness of EIS for identifying the RC circuit elements at various charging and discharging states. However, the study also acknowledges potential limitations of EIS in parametrisation due to its reliance on stable system conditions for accurate measurements and warns that the method may not capture the effect of endothermic reactions at high discharging currents. Moreover, EIS relies on expensive, specialised equipment such as the frequency response analyser or impedance analyser. Jackey et al. [23] introduce an automated layered approach to address the task of parameter estimation for lithium iron phosphate batteries. This method involves breaking down the estimation process into smaller tasks, focusing on a subset of experimental data to reduce complexity. The layered approach proved successful, achieving a mean residual error of 0.72 mV and a maximum error of 9.2 mV immediately after transient events. This technique enhances the accuracy of the ECM fitting, but the process is highly dependent on precise experimental setup and data quality, as it requires strictly controlled conditions to obtain data of high resolution and low noise. For instance, the poor resolution in voltage reading can lead to significant errors in parameter estimation, especially when dealing with small voltage changes.

The use of various algorithms and system control methods has emerged as a focal point in parameter extraction methods research, with this segment witnessing the greatest proliferation of literature in recent years. Jiang [24] presents a parameter fitting method utilising linear regression. This approach is highlighted for its simplicity and efficiency, with the study demonstrating <0.5 % error in the model. However, it is noted that this method relies on the quality and consistency of the test data, in a similar manner to the layered method. Zhang et al. [25] introduce a method that employs the decoupled weighted recursive least squares approach for accurate parameter estimation and SOC prediction, achieving <0.5 % error. However, the method's effectiveness is strongly dependent on the data quality, in a similar manner to the layered method. Xia et al. [26] introduce an approach for battery parameterisation using continuous-time techniques, offering enhanced accuracy and noise resilience, but with the trade-off of increased computational complexity and implementation challenges. These challenges arise due to the need for continuous data processing and the sophisticated algorithms involved in handling and interpreting real-time data streams. Yu et al. [27] present a method using the comprehensive particle swarm optimisation approach for dynamic parameter optimisation, achieving accurate Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) identification with minimal deviation, but potentially faces an inability to repeatedly optimise around a fixed point. This potential challenge arises from the inherent nature of particle swarm optimisation algorithms, which, while effective in exploring a broad solution space, can sometimes struggle with fine-tuning the optimisation process to consistently arrive at the same optimal point under varying conditions or over successive iterations.

In [28], a method tailored for the diverse dynamics of large-capacity lithium-ion batteries is introduced by Dai et al. The approach utilises two separate modules, one based on the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for slow dynamics and the other employing Recursive Least Squares (RLS) for fast dynamics, coupled through the voltage response of the slow dynamics. While the method excels in handling multi-scale dynamics, there is a need for precise coordination between the EKF and RLS modules during practical implementation, which adds complexity to the practical implementation of the method. Coordinating two distinct modules with different operational dynamics (EKF for slow dynamics and RLS for fast dynamics) requires careful synchronisation and adjustment to ensure they function harmoniously. Any misalignment or timing discrepancy between the EKF and RLS responses can lead to inaccuracies in the overall system performance, potentially compromising the effectiveness of the method in accurately tracking and predicting battery behaviour. In [29], a novel method for online parametrisation is proposed by Du et al., named the Information Appraisal Procedure-based Recursive Least Squares (IAP-RLS) algorithm. The method incorporates the Fisher-information-based Cramer–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) to appraise the accuracy of battery parameter estimation. This method addresses the limitations of traditional RLS methods, which are sensitive to noise corruption and require sufficient model excitation. The IAP-RLS technique evaluates measurement data, including noise-related information, to enable reliable parameter identification without the need for complex matrix inversion operations, thus making it suitable for online applications. The main advantages include enhanced accuracy, especially in the presence of noise and under varying excitation conditions, as well as reduced computational complexity. However, the manuscript does not explicitly discuss potential drawbacks, such as the robustness in extreme operational conditions or its scalability to different types of ECMs. In [30], a novel method for extracting parameters of the Thevenin ECMs is proposed by Hossain et al., focusing on their resilience to temperature effects. This approach models each RC circuit as a first-order linear time-invariant (LTI) system, identifying resistance and capacitance values as parameters of a standard LTI system, and compensating them for temperature variations. One significant advantage is the ability to adapt to a wide range of battery operating temperatures, a critical factor in battery management.

From the above, it can be observed that existing research in parameter extraction methods primarily emphasises the accuracy of parameters, with all methods capable of reducing voltage prediction errors to below 1 %. Additionally, some methods designed for online applications also consider real-time performance as one of their optimisation objects. However, some of the methods discussed in the literature are constrained by fixed experimental protocols and have high requirements for data quality, particularly those used in varied and dynamic environments, making them less suitable for generalising to different types of ECMs. The specificity of data needs and adaptability issues mean that these methods might struggle to maintain accuracy with less controlled or inconsistent data conditions. Additionally, the practical constraints of achieving and maintaining such high data quality can be challenging, especially for ECMs operating under diverse and unpredictable conditions. Furthermore, there is a notable gap regarding the development and application of half-cell ECMs. These models are critical for accurately understanding and analysing the behaviour of individual electrodes within a battery, which is essential for optimising overall battery performance and longevity [31]. The lack of focus on half-cell ECMs represents a significant limitation in current research, as these models provide valuable insights into electrode-specific phenomena, such as degradation mechanisms and interaction with the electrolyte, which are not fully captured by full-cell models [32]. Moreover, some of the methods only consider the impact of predefined temperature or current variations on parameter estimation. These methods require separate experiments for different current and temperature values, such that the parameter extraction process can be run on each data set in isolation. The ECM parameter values are subsequently merged in user-driven post-processing. This staged process makes these methods unusable in scenarios where battery test data is obtained under complex and uncontrollable temperature and current variations, such as during a real-life drive cycle. In such cases, parameter extraction becomes more challenging. Finally, all the literature focuses on discussing the methods themselves without considering their ease of use and user-friendliness.

Building upon the framework of battery parametrisation, we describe in this work the use of BatPar - a powerful, user-friendly, and application-oriented parameter extraction toolkit written in MATLAB 2022b. BatPar addresses some of the challenges in battery parametrisation, and it comes with specific contributions that make BatPar not just a tool but an advancement in the field of battery parametrisation:
1.
Adaptability to various experimental protocols: BatPar can handle data from standard protocols like GITT and HPPC, as well as variations of these and even data from operation drive cycles. As reviewed in the literature [23–30], most existing parametrisation methods require strict adherence to predetermined experimental procedures and specific data formats. In contrast, BatPar's parametrisation methodology is not limited to specific procedures or formats. This flexibility means that data from any experimental condition designed by researchers can be maximally utilised for parametrisation, eliminating constraints that parameter extraction typically places on experimental design.
2.
Compatibility with full-cell and half-cell ECMs: BatPar can perform parameter extraction for up to a third-order RC full-cell ECM, as well as for anode or cathode half-cell ECMs. Half-cell ECMs are crucial for accurately understanding and analysing individual electrode behaviours within a battery. These models provide insights into electrode-specific phenomena, such as degradation mechanisms and interactions with the electrolyte, which are not fully captured by full-cell models. To our knowledge, there are no existing parametrisation tools specifically targeting half-cell parametrisation, and BatPar addresses this gap.
3.
Handling of temperature and current dependencies: To obtain parameters at different temperatures and currents, traditional methods typically require setting multiple groups of different temperatures and currents, and then conducting separate experiments and parametrisation for each. BatPar's advantage lies in its ability to handle experimental data with complex and mixed changes in temperature and current, decoupling parameters for different temperatures and currents without demanding strict design or control of experimental processes.
4.
Quality Diagnostics of Parameters: A common concern post-parametrisation is the reliability of parameters. Validating parameter quality usually requires additional experimental and simulation comparisons. However, even if simulations do not correspond well with experiments, it may not necessarily be due to poor parameter quality; inaccuracies in battery modelling or noise in experimental data could also be factors. Thus, preliminary judgment of parameter quality is crucial to reduce uncertainty in subsequent troubleshooting. BatPar automatically assesses the quality of the identified OCV and R
0 upon the completion of parameter extraction. If anomalies are detected, they will be documented in the log file, to enable the user to investigate, which provides an early assessment of parameter quality and potentially reduces the need for additional experimental validation.

Furthermore, BatPar also incorporates four unique features aimed at enhancing software usability and user-friendliness:
1.
Graphical User Interface (GUI): BatPar offers a straightforward GUI (Fig. 5), which avoids the complexity of code-based operations, thus making it user-friendly for a wide audience.
2.
User-customisable parameter configuration: BatPar offers users the ability to tailor their parameter configuration to suit the specific ECM employed and the data collected. Options include setting the window sizes for SOC, temperature, and current, as well as specifying whether time constants are fixed or variable. Users can also choose to calculate the ohmic resistance (R
0 ) based on the voltage rising or falling edge, crop the input dataset to focus on a specific range of data rather than encompassing the entire dataset, and input known values rather than self-consistently fit for OCV and/or R
0 .
3.
Efficient post-processing: The parameter extraction results are automatically saved in the formats of Excel spreadsheets, MATLAB plots, and a log file, making it easier for users to collect and review the results.
4.
Batch processing: Considering the time-consuming nature of manually configuring the GUI for multiple datasets, BatPar includes a batch processing feature. Users can place all their datasets in a single folder, and run BatPar to read and parameterise all successively.

The MATLAB codes of BatPar are available on GitHub, with affiliated user (12 slides) and developer (68-page document) manuals. Through the abovementioned features, BatPar aims to offer a comprehensive, one-stop solution for parameter extraction, streamlining both academic research and industrial use, wherever battery ECM parametrisation is needed. The rest of the manuscript is organised as follows: Section 2 explains the methodology BatPar uses for parameter extraction, Section 3 introduces BatPar from a user perspective, Section 4 presents the results of parametrisation and Section 5 provides a summary.

In this section, it is assumed that the nominal battery capacity and operating voltage range are known, and the voltage response of the battery under a specified current profile in the time domain has already been obtained from experiments. We recommend that the current pulse adopted should have an amplitude exceeding 1 A. This is critical to mitigating the influence of cycler noise, ensuring that the data collected is reflective of the actual battery behaviour rather than artifacts of the testing equipment. Moreover, we recommend a resolution of at least 10 mV in the voltage response, ensuring that the voltage curve obtained possesses adequate resolution for precise parameter extraction. The methodology used for parameter fit is described here for a second-order RC ECM, as an example, as shown in Fig. 1 , with governing equation as shown in Eq. (1):
(1)
V = OCV − I
R 0
−
V 1
−
V 2
d
V 1
dt
= −
V 1
R 1
C 1
+
I
C 1
d
V 2
dt
= −
V 2
R 2
C 2
+
I
C 2
where V is the terminal voltage of the battery, I is the current, V 1 and V 2 are the voltages, R
1 and R
2 are the resistances, C
1 and C
2 are the capacitances of the first and the second RC pair, respectively [33]. The parameters to be identified are OCV, R
0 , R
1 , R
2 , C
1 , and C
2 under user-defined SOCs.

BatPar consists of approximately 30 MATLAB files and supporting files, with interactions among these files illustrated in Fig. 2 . Initially, users need to enter their parametrisation configurations into the GUI, such as whether to extract parameters for discharge or charge. Users must also choose whether to perform batch processing on multiple data files or parametrisation on a single file. Subsequently, BatPar reads the data files and preprocesses them. This preprocessing involves segregating the data by charge or discharge and SOC intervals, forming a 2D array. Then BatPar first attempts to parametrise OCV and R
0 using analytical methods whenever possible, and the numerical methods are employed only when analytical solutions are unfeasible. Analytical methods are preferred because they require fewer computational resources and can avoid potential errors and instabilities that might arise from numerical methods. However, using analytical methods to extract OCV assumes the presence of long relaxation periods in the experimental data or very low currents throughout the experimental process; to extract R
0 analytically, the experimental data must include pulses. If these conditions are not met, OCV or R
0 have to be extracted using numerical methods. The extraction of OCV or R
0 is further detailed in Sections 2.2 and 2.3.

Then, if the user has configured temperature-dependent or current-dependent parametrisation, the data will be further reformed to potentially form a 4D cell indexed by charge/discharge, SOC, temperature, and currents. The temperature dependence reform is relatively straightforward and can directly index the experimental data based on the temperature ranges set by the user. In contrast, the reform for current dependence is more complex. It not only needs to index the data within the current ranges specified by the user but also includes data around the pulse edges, as shown in Fig. 3 . These pulse edges contain crucial information for correcting R
0 , which can be quickly calculated analytically if accurately identified. Otherwise, R
0 can only be calculated using analytical methods, which are slow and potentially error-prone. Subsequently, if OCV or R
0 has been determined by the above processes, they will be imported as known quantities into the ECM model corresponding to Fig. 1, and the remaining parameters R
1 , R
2 , C
1 , and C
2 will then be solved numerically. If not, OCV or R
0 , along with the remaining Rs and Cs, will be treated as unknowns in numerical fitting. The fitting algorithm used in BatPar is fmincon [34], which seeks to find the optimal values for the unknowns so that the ECM-predicted voltage matches the experimentally measured voltage as closely as possible. The cost function for measuring this match is the root-mean-square-error (RMSE) between the ECM-predicted voltage and the experimentally measured voltage.

The reason for choosing fmincon is that it is well suited for finding the minimum of a constrained nonlinear multivariable function, which aligns well with the nature of parametrisation. Firstly, BatPar involves constraints related to parameters that must stay within realistic physical limits (e.g., Rs and Ces must be positive). Fmincon allows these constraints to be incorporated directly into the optimisation process, ensuring the solutions are not only optimal but also physically plausible. Secondly, fmincon is effective for handling nonlinear objective function that needs to encapsulate various elements of the battery model. Lastly, while sensitive to initial guesses, fmincon can converge to an optimal solution efficiently, even if the initial guesses are not very close to the true parameter values, provided that the problem is well-posed and the model accurately reflects the physical system.

After obtaining the parameters under all conditions of charge/discharge, SOC, temperature, and current, these parameters will be imported into the ECM model, which then preforms simulation under the exact input conditions used in the experiments. The simulation data will be compared with the experimental data for validation. After validation, BatPar will generate plots and tables of parameters. The methodology above is primarily for full-cell parametrisation; the methodology for half-cell parametrisation slightly differs and will be specifically introduced in Section 2.5.

There are three methods for extracting the OCV:
1)
If the experimental data includes sufficiently long relaxation periods (e.g., relaxation >4 h), the OCV corresponding to an SOC state can usually be identified with the end voltage of the relaxation period, as identified in Fig. 3.
2)
If the experimental data is obtained at a very low current rate (e.g., current rate < C/20), the terminal voltage of the battery, also called in this situation as the pseudo-OCV, can be approximated as the OCV.
3)
In cases where neither of the first two conditions is met, the OCV has to be treated as an unknown variable and determined numerically by fitting to data, along with the other parameters, by an optimisation algorithm for curve fitting.

Depending on the nature of their experimental data, users can choose to use any of the three methods to obtain values for the OCV. Whenever possible, the first method is recommended due to its direct and natural approach to capturing OCV in a stable state, reflecting the battery's inherent characteristics without the influence of external factors. The second method offers a practical alternative when longer relaxations are not feasible, while the third method provides a solution when the experimental setup constraints prevent the use of the first two methods. The choice among these methods depends on the specific conditions and constraints of the experiment, such as total available time, with the aim to achieve the most accurate representation of the OCV for the given circumstances.

The R
0 values can be obtained in two ways:
1)
If the experimental data includes current pulses, R
0 can be calculated from the ratio of the voltage response to a sharp (ideally instantaneous) change, i.e., R
0 = dV/dI. Building on this, BatPar allows users the option to calculate R
0 using either the rising edge or the falling edge of the pulse, as shown in Fig. 3. The R
0 values calculated from the two methods have been found to differ significantly [35]. With this method, the accuracy is significantly constrained by the frequency of the data sampling used by the battery cycler in the experiment. High-frequency sampling captures the nuances of voltage changes more accurately, especially during the transient moments of the current pulse. Lower sampling frequencies might not adequately capture these rapid changes, leading to less precise R
0 values. Some battery cyclers capture the rising edge of the pulse more accurately, while others are more accurate with the falling edge of the pulse [35].
2)
If the first condition is not met, R
0 can only be treated as an unknown parameter and obtained numerically along with the rest of the parameters. A fitting algorithm can be used to optimise the discrepancies between the observed and simulated battery voltage as a whole.

Users are free to choose between the analytical method and the numerical method for determining R
0 . However, it is advised to opt for the first method, whenever experimental data permits.

The remaining parameters, R
1 , R
2 , C
1 , and C
2 , are treated as unknown variables and are fitted numerically. As previously mentioned, if OCV and/or R
0 cannot be solved through the ideal methods, they will be included along with R
1 , R
2 , C
1 , and C
2 for global fitting solutions. This means that up to six variables may be involved in the curve fitting process simultaneously. In such cases, BatPar treats parameter extraction as a purely numerical optimisation problem involving up to six fitting coefficients, eliminating the reliance on analytical methods for solutions. This flexibility also eliminates the need for specific current pulses or long relaxation periods in the experimental data, thereby enabling BatPar to work with non-standard experimental protocols, such as drive cycles, or even randomly generated data for parameter extraction.

BatPar incorporates three strategies to mitigate potential overfitting. Firstly, BatPar implements a hierarchical parametrisation strategy that prioritises the extraction of parameters with the most significant impact on the model output, namely the open-circuit voltage OCV and ohmic resistance R
0 . When experimental data permits, the two parameters are first parametrised using analytical methods, avoiding fitting and thereby eliminating the risk of overfitting. Subsequently, BatPar fits the remaining parameters. This strategy not only boosts the parametrisation efficiency but also minimises overfitting risk by sidestepping unnecessary complexity of the fitting model. Secondly, the quality diagnosis feature within BatPar is instrumental in post-parametrisation overfitting detection. By comparing fitted parameters against known benchmarks, BatPar can identify potential discrepancies suggestive of overfitting. Upon identifying such discrepancies, BatPar alerts users to inspect and potentially modify the parametrisation settings or reassess the quality of experimental data (Section 4.2). Lastly, BatPar allows users to specify their upper limits for the time constant and resistance of each RC circuit, offering an additional safeguard against overfitting. This feature lets users maintain parameters within physically justifiable limits, preventing the fitting model from overly conforming to the peculiarities of the dataset.

If a reference electrode is inserted between the cathode and anode of a full-cell, the cathode and anode each forms a half-cell with the reference electrode, allowing the full-cell to be viewed as a combination of these two half-cells. Half-cell models help in understanding electrode-specific phenomena such as ion exchange, electrode potential, and electrochemical reactions independently of the full-cell configuration. Each half-cell can be regarded as having a set of equivalent circuits similar to those of the full-cell, as shown in Fig. 4 . Both the cathode half-cell and anode half-cell features an open circuit voltage source, ohmic resistance, and RC circuit(s).

The parametrisation methodology for Rs and Cs of half-cells can follow those of a full-cell, as described in Sections 2.3 and 2.4. The main difference between parameterisation for half-cells and full-cells lies in the voltage aspect. For example, the OCV of the cathode half-cell is higher than the OCV of a full-cell at the same SOC, but it does not exceed twice the maximum voltage of the full cell. If the cathode's OCV is considered positive, then the anode's OCV is negative, and the absolute value of the anode OCV does not exceed the maximum voltage of the full cell. Accurately and adequately configuring voltage boundary conditions during parametrisation not only ensures that the derived OCV has a monotonic relationship with SOC but also avoids ineffective optimisation paths, thereby accelerating the convergence speed of the fitting algorithm. To this end, Table 1 compares the voltage boundary conditions configured in BatPar, where the boundary conditions for the full-cell, cathode, and anode differ, as do the boundary conditions for charge and discharge. In the table, Vmax and Vmin are the maximum and minimum voltages of the cell, which for the LG M50 cell are 4.2 V and 2.5 V, respectively. Voc represents OCV, V the terminal voltage, and Vi the voltage across one RC circuit.

BatPar incorporates four strategies to inherently address the potential variability introduced by different experimental conditions, and more importantly, ensure that the adaptability to various experimental conditions does not introduce inconsistencies in parametrisation. Firstly, BatPar employs a preprocessing stage for the input data and standardises it regardless of the experimental conditions under which it was collected. This preprocessing includes noise filtering, outlier removal, and normalisation of the data (Sections 3 and 4). Secondly, when users indicate the necessity to consider temperature and/or current dependencies, BatPar can adapt the parametrisation process to specifically account for these known dependencies. This means that even if experiments are conducted under varying conditions, BatPar can compensate for these variations, leading to consistent and reliable parameter values (Section 4.4). Thirdly, the fmincon algorithm used can handle non-linear dependencies inherent in ECMs, ensuring that the optimisation process is not only efficient but also resilient to variability (Section 2.3). Lastly, BatPar integrates a quality diagnostics functionality to enable users to evaluate the quality of the extracted parameters through visual, quantitative metrics (Section 4.2).

User interactions can be facilitated through the GUI depicted in Fig. 5 . The GUI consists of 40 fields open to user interaction, labeled from (1) to (40), each serving specific functionalities. Fields labeled (1) to (15) are mandatory inputs. Fields (16) to (35) are optional inputs, which can be left empty depending on the specific needs for parametrisation. Lastly, Fields (36) to (40) are buttons that trigger different actions of BatPar. Each of the fields is explained below.

Field (1) requires the user to specify the storage path for the experimental dataset(s). BatPar will search for all files with the .csv or .xlsx extensions in that path and then carry out independent parametrisation for each file.

Fields (2)–(6) are used to indicate which columns in the dataset record the index, time, voltage, current, and temperature, respectively. In this way, BatPar can extract information from the corresponding columns for parametrisation.
Field (7) is used to define the polarity of the current. For example, some battery cyclers may define the current as positive when the battery is discharging, while others may define it as negative.

In Field (8), the rated capacity of the battery is required, which will be used in the calculation of the battery's SOC based on ampere-hour integration. Since battery cyclers do not output SOC, SOC has to be calculated separately using the current and capacity. If the inputted capacity is inaccurate, such as being too low, BatPar could generate parameters for SOC values >100 % or <0 %. This means BatPar does not automatically restrict SOC unless the user utilises the functionality in Field (16) to set upper and lower limits on SOC.

Fields (9)–(10) require the operating voltage range as specified in the battery specifications. Data falling outside of this voltage range will be treated as noise and discarded. It is important to note that this approach may not account for changes in the operating voltage range due to battery degradation over time. For aged batteries, the real operating voltage range used in practice, rather than the nominal range specified in the specifications, should be used.

For Field (11), if the user selects that the battery was ever fully charged, this implies that the battery's SOC had reached 100 % at some point. In that case, 100 % will be used as the upper bound for SOC, and ampere-hour integration will be employed to calculate the SOC for the entire experiment. Conversely, if the user indicates that the battery was never fully charged but was fully discharged, 0 will be used as the lower bound for SOC. From this minimum value, ampere-hour integration will be used to compute the SOC throughout the entire experiment. However, in cases where the battery was neither fully charged nor fully discharged, determining an accurate SOC becomes challenging. While it is true that any reference point could theoretically be used to estimate SOC, the current implementation of BatPar does not support this functionality. Therefore, for the current functionality of BatPar, it is necessary to design the experiment such that the battery is either fully charged or fully discharged at least once. This is deemed to not be a major drawback, considering the type of experiments usually run for battery parametrisation, and the fact that an as wide as possible window of operation is usually targeted for the beginning of life parametrisation.

In field (12) the user must choose the number of RC pairs in the ECM, thus defining the set of unknown parameters. For example, if the user opts for a first-order RC ECM and enters ‘1’ in Field (12), then BatPar fits values for OCV, R
0 , R
1 , and C
1 .

Field (13) enables the user to customise the intervals of SOC. For instance, if the user inputs 0.05, BatPar would obtain parameter values at SOC levels of [100 %, 95 %, 90 %, …, 10 %, 5 %, 0 %], provided that the experimental data covers these SOC levels. For SOC values not covered in the experiment or those with insufficient data to support parametrisation, BatPar will automatically skip those SOC points and note this in the log file. Considering that each RC circuit has three unknowns (R, C, and the initial voltage value of the RC circuit), an N-th order RC circuit will have 3 N unknowns. Besides these, the entire equivalent circuit contains two more unknowns, OCV and R
0 , resulting in a total of (3 N + 2) unknowns. This number of records is expected in the experimental data. Considering the potential linear relationships between records which may render them ineffective, an additional multiplication factor of 2 is required. Therefore, to uniquely determine all parameters at a specific SOC, there must be at least (N*3 + 2)*2 experimental records at that SOC; otherwise, it will be considered as ‘insufficient data’.

Field (14) relates to the time constant (Tau) associated with the RC pairs. Theoretically, the value of Tau should be a constant that does not vary with SOC [36]. However, practical tests often show variations in Tau at different SOCs, and a better fit can be usually obtained with a varying Tau. In BatPar, users have the option to constrain Tau to be constant or to allow it to vary with the SOC. Opting for the latter allows for more accurate curve fitting but also increases BatPar's computational runtime by approximately 50 %.

Field (15) allows the user to set whether BatPar calculates the parameter R
0 using the rising edge (also known as the pulse head) or the falling edge (also known as the pulse end) of a current pulse. Consideration should be given as described in Section 2 to details in the experimental data or type of cycler used.
Field (16) is designed for situations in which the user only wishes to parametrise within a specific range of SOC, rather than spanning the complete range covered in the experiments.
Fields (17)–(19) offer users the flexibility to select only a subset of the experimental data inputted for parametrisation, rather than using the entire dataset.

Fields (20)–(21) allow users to import an external SOC-OCV table, and Fields (22)–(23) permit the importation of a SOC-R
0 table, treating OCV or R
0 as a priori known values.

Field (24) accommodates cases where the user is only interested in parameterising OCV or R
0 , without an interest in parameterising the other ECM elements.

Fields (25)–(27) are designed to extend the parametrisation to consider the dependency of parameters on current in addition to SOC. Once Field (25) is enabled, the user must specify in Field (26) the current values (in Ampere) at which parametrisation should occur. Additionally, Field (27) allows the user to define the allowable error range for these current values. For example, if the user inputs ‘2, 8’ in Field (26) and ‘0.2’ in Field (27), BatPar will first select all data from the input dataset that falls within the current ranges of [1.8 A, 2.2 A] and [7.8 A, 8.2 A]. Then, it performs parametrisation fitting separately within these two current intervals. The identified parameters are considered as the parameters at 2 A and 8 A, respectively.

Fields (28)–(30) are designed to extend the parametrisation to consider the dependency of parameters on temperature in addition to SOC. Once Field (28) is enabled, the user must specify in Field (29) the temperature values (in °C) at which parametrisation should occur. Additionally, Field (30) allows the user to define the allowable error range for these temperature values.

Fields (31)–(35) allow the user to update various internal boundaries within BatPar. These boundaries set upper limits for the Tau and R values of the RC pairs, with the goal of accelerating the parametrisation process by preventing the iteration of the solution space from diverging to infinity. The preset upper limits for Tau and R are based on observations across a wide range of cells, but they may not be universally applicable. If users find that the preset values are too restrictive, they can incrementally adjust the upper limits for Tau and R through a trial-and-error method. This can facilitate more accurate parametrisation.
Field (36) specifies whether the input dataset(s) relate to a full-cell or to a half-cell cathode/anode.

Field (37) designates whether the parametrisation is for charging or discharging, as parameters may differ under these two conditions [37]. BatPar will filter the dataset based on this selection for either charging or discharging for parametrisation.

Fields (38)–(40) indicate whether the parametrisation is for a single dataset (single run for one file) or multiple datasets (batch run for multiple files). After configuring all previous fields, the button in Field (38) initiates the parametrisation process. In the single run mode, only one dataset may be present in the specified storage path. When there are multiple datasets requiring parametrisation, the user should click on Field (39) or (40). Field (39) uses the configuration entered in the GUI for each dataset, meaning that the parametrisation requirements for each dataset must be the same and come from the GUI. However, sometimes, the parametrisation requirements for each dataset are different. In this case, the user should use the input table shown in Fig. 6 . Users can use this table to configure different parametrisation requirements for each dataset. Upon clicking the button for Field (40), BatPar does not use the configuration entered in the GUI; instead, it uses the input table for targeted parametrisation for each dataset.

BatPar displays real-time progress in the command window during its operation, including the dataset currently undergoing parametrisation, and the SOC being parameterised in that dataset (as well as current and/or temperature, if multiple currents and/or temperatures are involved), as shown in Fig. 7 . These real-time progress updates are saved in the log file after the parametrisation process is complete. The results and post-processing after parametrisation are explained in Section 4.

The runtime of BatPar depends on the scale of experimental data and the configuration of equivalent circuit. For a typical case, such as a GITT full-cell discharge experiment where the voltage data resolution is approximately 1 mV and the configured equivalent circuit is a second-order RC, the unknown parameters to be identified include all of OCV, R0, R1, R2, C1, and C2, with the SOC interval set at 2 % and the Tau constants assumed to be invariant. When processed on an Intel Core i7 processor, the runtime for this scenario is approximately 12 min.

In the single run mode, as depicted in Fig. 8 , BatPar creates a folder with the same name as the dataset being parametrised in the “\Result” directory of the root folder. Upon completion of the run, BatPar stores the parametrisation results in this folder in the form of one log file (.txt), two data matrices (.mat), one Excel spreadsheet (.xlsx), and numerous MATLAB plots, as shown in Fig. 8(a). Fig. 8(a)-(d) display the parametrisation outcomes for an LG M50 cell (4.85 Ah, 2.5 V–4.2 V) [38] following a discharge GITT test at conditions of 25 °C forced air cooling (in thermal chamber) and 0.5 C current rate. Fig. 8(b) shows the parameter values corresponding to various SOCs, Fig. 8(c) depicts the parametrised OCV as a function of SOC, and Fig. 8(d) provides a comparison between the observed voltage and the simulated voltage. In batch run mode, BatPar will create separate folders for each dataset, named accordingly, and save result files that are similar to those shown in Fig. 8.

These results can be further used to diagnose the quality of the parameters. Fig. 8(d) serves as a comprehensive quality assessment for all parameters; if the measured and simulated voltages show good enough agreement (where what is good enough is left to be appraised by the user), the user can consider the overall parameter quality high. Additionally, the RMSE between the measured and simulated voltages across different SOCs is quantified in the command window or log file, as seen in Fig. 7. This facilitates further investigation into which SOCs have comparatively poorer parameter quality. For the parametrised OCV, BatPar automatically checks whether the OCV trend with SOC is strictly non-decreasing [39]. If it is not, a statement will be output in both the command window and log file: ‘Parameterised OCV is not monotonic at SOC = 0.34 0.38 – You may consider smoothing OCV’, serving as a user alert. This choice is left to the user, as some battery chemistries are known to have pronounced voltage plateaus (lithium ion phosphate) and even localised voltage increase with decreasing SOC (lithium sulfur). BatPar automatically checks whether the R
0 with SOC roughly forms an ‘L’ shape, as shown in Fig. 9(a) [40]. Outliers that do not conform to this shape are flagged to the user, in an output statement such as ‘Noteworthy outlier(s) detected for R
0
at SOC = 0.6 0.8 - You may consider smoothing R
0
’. For R
1 , R
2 , C
1 , and C
2 , BatPar does not offer quality diagnosis prompts to the user, as these resistances and capacitances can exhibit various shapes. However, as a rule of thumb, these parameters should resemble Fig. 9(b), exhibiting abrupt changes only at low SOCs, and ideally not many unphysical spikes in the middle range [41]. The same testing data was used in Fig. 9 as in Fig. 8.

As previously described, BatPar can handle various experimental protocols, including arbitrary tests such as the operation drive cycles. Fig. 10 showcases the parametrisation results derived from a drive cycle test, which reduced the cell SOC from 100 % to approximately 64 %. This test was conducted at an average current rate of 0.5 C and a temperature of 25 °C, using the same LG M50 cell. Fig. 10(a) compares the experimentally measured and the ECM-predicted cell voltage. The overall discrepancy between them is within 18 mV, indicating that the parameters obtained can accurately reflect the cell's performance under drive cycle testing conditions. Fig. 10(b) to (e) compares the OCV, R
0 , R
1 , R
2 extracted from the drive cycle test with those extracted from the standard GITT test discussed in Section 4.1. The close agreement between the two sets of data in Fig. 10(b) demonstrates BatPar's capability to extract accurate OCV from arbitrary test data. Fig. 10(c) to (e) display the parametrised resistances, where the difference in R
1 values between the standard test and drive cycle test are minimal. However, R
0 is about half of what is observed in the standard test, and R
2 is twice as high. Since the drive cycle test does not include relaxation periods, the extracted R
0 tends to be underestimated, and R
2 overestimated. Despite these variations, the linear sum of R
0 , R
1 , and R
2 remain consistent, allowing the parameters obtained from the drive cycle test to accurately account for the cell's behaviour under such conditions. Based on these observations, while BatPar is capable of extracting parameters from arbitrary tests, we still recommend using standard tests for parametrisation when possible. Extracting parameters from arbitrary tests without standard conditions may lead to offsets among R
0 , R
1 , and R
2 , although they still collectively reflect the cell's behaviour adequately.

As previously mentioned, BatPar is capable of determining parameters at user-specified temperatures and/or currents under complex temperature and/or current experimental conditions. If the user specifies only temperature, parameters corresponding to various temperatures and SOCs will be obtained, as illustrated in Fig. 11(a), where the raw test data was collected from the discharge GITT test involving five temperatures (15 °C, 25 °C, 35 °C, 45 °C, and 55 °C) and one single current rate (1 C). If only current is specified, BatPar will yield parameters for various currents and SOCs, as shown in Fig. 11(b), where the raw test data was collected from the discharge GITT test involving four current rates (0.3 C, 0.5 C, 1 C, and 2 C) and one single temperature (25 °C). When both temperature and current are specified by the user, BatPar will provide parameters first for various currents and SOCs, and subsequently for different temperatures, as depicted in Fig. 11(c), where the raw test data was collected from the discharge GITT test involving two current rates (0.3 C and 5 C) and two temperatures (10 °C and 25 °C). The test data for Fig. 11 was obtained from the LG M50 cells.

As previously stated, BatPar can also parametrise half-cell configurations, be it the cathode or anode. Fig. 12 displays the parametrisation results for a cell's cathode and anode. In this case, simulated half-cell virtual experimental data for a charge GITT test is performed using the physics-based models of the LG M50 cell in PybaMM [42], at 5 °C constant temperature and 2 C current rate (9.7 A). Fig. 12(a) illustrates the comparison between the virtual experimental and simulated voltages for the cathode, while Fig. 12(b) shows the corresponding data for the anode half-cells. From the figures, it is evident that BatPar's parametrisation accuracy extends effectively to half-cell configurations.

This paper introduces BatPar, an all-in-one MATLAB-based toolkit designed to streamline the intricate process of battery parametrisation. Leveraging both analytical and numerical methods, BatPar provides an adaptive, flexible framework compatible with both standard and non-standard experimental protocols and various ECM configurations. Currently, BatPar supports up to third-order full- or half-cell models, with the potential for easy expansion to other ECM types. This toolkit uniquely extends the traditional parametrisation scope to consider temperature and current dependencies. Equipped with an intuitive GUI, user-customisable settings to tailor parameter configuration, efficient post-processing features to automatically collect and plot the results, and diagnostic capabilities to assess the quality of the identified OCV and R
0 , BatPar offers a comprehensive solution that can significantly expedite academic research and industrial applications in the realm of battery technology. The capability of BatPar to work with full-cell and half-cell ECMs further augments its applicability, as half-cell models are crucial for investigating individual electrode behaviours in battery research, essential for enhancing electrode materials and understanding degradation mechanisms unique to each electrode. The batch processing capability of BatPar is particularly beneficial for high-throughput data analysis, saving time and resources. Furthermore, the quality diagnostics integrated into BatPar aids users in identifying and rectifying anomalies in parameter data, thus enhancing the reliability of the parametrisation process.

The open-source availability of BatPar, complete with comprehensive user and developer manuals, is expected to facilitate the broader adoption and improvement by users of this toolkit, catalysing advances in battery management system, energy storage solution, and electric vehicle development. Future work will focus on enhancing the computational efficiency of the numerical methods employed and expanding the toolkit's capabilities to include more complex battery models, such as for degradation, as well as integration with other diagnostic tools.
The source codes, a user manual and a developer manual of BatPar can be found on GitHub via the link: https://github.com/BatParDeveloperTeam/BatPar.

Tao Zhu: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ruben Tomlin: Software, Methodology, Conceptualization. Carlos Garcia: Validation, Investigation. Sunil Rawat: Validation, Formal analysis. Thomas Holland: Software, Methodology. Gregory Offer: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Monica Marinescu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the Innovate UK (Power-up project, grant number 10007479; WIZer, grant number 104427; GENESIS, grant number 10007488), UK Engineering and Physical Science Research Council (Multi-Scale Modelling, grant number EP/S003053/1; EPSRC CASE, grant number EP/W524323/1), and the Faraday Institution (Multi-Scale Modelling project, grant number FIRG003, FIRG025, and FIRG059).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Real-time estimation of negative electrode potential and state of charge of lithium-ion battery based on a half-cell-level equivalent circuit model
J. Energy Storage
